worth_it_explanation: Floor strategy sets the price below which an impression goes unsold, so it controls the trade between fill rate and the value of each impression. A floor that leans entirely on automation, or one flat number applied across every ad unit, country, and device, can leave revenue on the table without anyone noticing. The check is a review of the pricing rules a publisher already has in Google Ad Manager, so it costs reading time rather than rebuild time, and the finding points to a concrete pricing decision the publisher can test.
intro: Floor pricing decides the minimum price Google Ad Manager (GAM) accepts for an impression in the open auction, which sets where the line falls between filling more impressions and protecting the value of each one. This subcategory is about the floor strategy itself, the values chosen and how they are applied, rather than the unified pricing rules mechanism that carries them. These checks confirm that the floor strategy is a deliberate, evidence-based choice rather than automation accepted by default, and that the floors reflect the price differences across the publisher's inventory.

audit_checks.title: Optimized pricing is accepted without evaluating it against fixed floors
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Optimized pricing lets Google set the floor automatically, which often raises revenue, but it does not always match a publisher's strategy, for example protecting a premium price point. Accepting it without comparing it to a fixed-floor baseline leaves the choice untested, so a publisher cannot tell whether the automated floor is helping or trading away inventory value on a slice of inventory.

educate.base:
Google Ad Manager (GAM) gives a publisher more than one way to set a floor in a pricing rule. A fixed floor is a number the publisher chooses and GAM applies as written. Optimized floor prices instead hand the floor to a Google machine-learning model that adjusts it per query to balance fill and value, and target CPM works similarly by setting the floor dynamically around an average price the publisher names. The automated options often raise revenue, and Google's own guidance frames them as a way to find the price that fills the impression without giving the inventory away.

The risk is not that automation is wrong. The risk is accepting it without a comparison. An automated floor optimizes toward the objective Google built it for, which is yield, and that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy. A publisher protecting a premium price point on a sponsorship-adjacent placement, or holding a deliberate floor to keep a direct-sold rate intact, may find the automated floor clears impressions below the price they meant to protect. Without a baseline to measure against, the publisher has no way to see that happening.

Google Ad Manager supports running this comparison. A publisher can apply optimized or target CPM pricing to a slice of inventory, hold a fixed floor on a comparable slice, and read the revenue, fill rate, and average price across both. That turns the floor choice into a measured result rather than a default that was never questioned.

A few signs point to optimized pricing being accepted rather than evaluated:
  • Pricing rules use optimized floor prices or target CPM across all inventory with no fixed-floor comparison on record
  • No experiment or before-and-after read exists showing how the automated floor performed against a fixed baseline
  • The publisher cannot say why the automated option was chosen beyond it being the default suggestion
You may want to confirm that the optimized-versus-fixed choice was made on evidence rather than by default. One approach is to hold a fixed-floor baseline on part of the inventory long enough to compare it against the automated floor on the rest.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, premium placements such as a homepage takeover slot or a sponsorship-adjacent unit often carry a price the publisher set on purpose to protect direct-sold value. You may want to keep a fixed-floor baseline on those units while testing optimized pricing elsewhere, so the comparison shows whether automation respects the premium or clears below it.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages can command higher prices than editorial pages because the audience is closer to a purchase. You may want to compare optimized pricing against a fixed floor on the commercial pages specifically, rather than judging it on a blended average that the lower-value editorial inventory pulls down.

learn_more.source_title: Optimize floor prices in pricing rules (Beta)

investigate.intro: Review which pricing rules use optimized or target CPM pricing versus a fixed floor, and look for any test result or before-and-after read that compares them.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Inventory', then 'Pricing rules', and list the active unified pricing rules.
For each rule, open it and note whether its floor is set as a fixed price, optimized floor prices, or target CPM.
Record which slices of inventory run on an automated floor and which, if any, run on a fixed floor that could serve as a comparison baseline.
Ask the publisher whether any experiment or before-and-after comparison was run, and record what the result showed and when it was measured.

gam_reporting.label: Google Ad Manager reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by the pricing rule or the inventory it targets, with revenue, impressions, fill rate, and average eCPM.
Compare a slice running an automated floor against a comparable slice on a fixed floor, if one exists, to see whether the automated floor raised revenue or cleared at a lower price.
Note whether the data supports the current floor choice or whether no fixed-floor slice exists to compare against.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per pricing rule, whether the floor is fixed, optimized, or target CPM, and whether any comparison between an automated floor and a fixed baseline exists with a result on record. A setup where the optimized-versus-fixed choice is deliberate and backed by a comparison passes. A setup where automated floors are applied across inventory with no fixed-floor comparison and no stated reason for the choice is a fail recorded with the rules and inventory affected.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor type for each pricing rule and whether an optimized-versus-fixed comparison exists above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} applies optimized or target CPM floor pricing without a fixed-floor comparison on record, so the choice between automated and fixed floors has not been tested. Optimized pricing in Google Ad Manager (GAM) sets the floor to maximize yield, which often helps, but that objective does not always match a publisher's own strategy, such as protecting a premium price point, and without a baseline the difference goes unmeasured. {{voice_recommend}} holding a fixed-floor baseline on a slice of comparable inventory, running the automated floor on the rest, and comparing revenue, fill rate, and average price across both before settling on a floor strategy. After the comparison, keep whichever floor the data supports for each slice of inventory.

audit_checks.title: Floors are not segmented by inventory, geography, or device
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A single flat floor across all inventory ignores that some placements, countries, and devices clear at higher prices than others. A flat floor either underprices the strong inventory, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the weak inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot serve inventory whose value varies.

educate.base:
A floor price works best when it matches what the impression is worth, and impressions are not worth the same across a publisher's inventory. An above-the-fold unit on a high-engagement page clears higher than a unit far down a thin page. Traffic from a high-spending country clears higher than traffic from a market where advertiser demand is thin. Desktop, mobile web, and in-app inventory each carry their own demand and their own typical clearing price. A floor set as one flat number across all of this cannot fit inventory whose value varies along every one of those lines.

The cost of a flat floor cuts both ways. Set it to the average and it sits below the strong inventory, so impressions that could have cleared higher clear at the floor and the difference is left uncollected. Set it higher to protect the strong inventory and it sits above the weak inventory, so impressions that would have sold at a lower price go unsold instead. Either way, one number serves the middle and misprices the ends.

Google Ad Manager (GAM) lets pricing rules target the dimensions that move clearing price, so a publisher can set different floors for different ad units, geographies, and device categories rather than one floor for everything. The audit question is whether the floors in place reflect those value differences or whether a single flat floor is doing work that several segmented floors should do.

A few signs point to floors that are not segmented:
  • One pricing rule with a single floor covers all inventory, with no breakout by ad unit, geography, or device
  • Reporting shows average clearing prices that differ widely across placements, countries, or devices while the floor stays the same for all of them
  • Premium placements clear at or near the same floor as filler inventory, which suggests the floor is not protecting their value
You may want to confirm that the floors reflect the price differences the inventory actually shows. One approach is to read average clearing price by ad unit, geography, and device, then set floors that follow those differences rather than a single number across the board.

site_type_overlays.publishing: On a media site, a homepage or section-front placement usually clears higher than an article-footer unit, and audience from high-demand markets clears higher than audience from thin ones. You may want to set floors by placement and geography so the premium inventory holds its value while the lower-value inventory keeps a floor low enough to stay filled.
site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On a commerce-content site, product and category pages reach an audience closer to a purchase and clear higher than editorial pages, and device matters because mobile shoppers and desktop shoppers carry different demand. You may want to segment floors so the commercial, higher-intent inventory carries a higher floor than the editorial inventory rather than sharing one number.

investigate.intro: Review the floor values across ad units, geographies, and device categories to see whether they are differentiated or whether one flat floor covers everything.

gam_ui.label: Google Ad Manager UI
steps: Open 'Inventory', then 'Pricing rules', and list the active unified pricing rules with their floor values.
For each rule, open its targeting and note which ad units, geographies, and device categories it covers and at what floor.
Record whether the floors differ across ad unit, geography, and device, or whether a single floor applies across all inventory with no breakout.

gam_reporting.label: Google Ad Manager reporting
steps: Build a report broken out by ad unit, then by country, then by device category, with average eCPM and fill rate.
Compare the average clearing price across those dimensions to see how much it varies between strong and weak inventory.
Note any case where clearing prices differ widely but the floor stays the same, which points to inventory that a segmented floor could price more accurately.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the floor values across ad units, geographies, and device categories alongside the average clearing price for each. Floors that follow the value differences the reporting shows, with higher floors on inventory that clears higher and lower floors on inventory that clears lower, pass. A single flat floor applied across inventory whose clearing prices differ widely is a fail recorded with the dimensions where the floor and the value diverge.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the floor values and the average clearing price by ad unit, geography, and device above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}} applies a single flat floor across inventory whose value differs by ad unit, geography, and device. A flat floor either underprices the strong inventory, leaving revenue uncollected, or overprices the weak inventory, leaving impressions unsold, so one number cannot fit inventory that clears at different prices. {{voice_recommend}} reading the average clearing price by ad unit, geography, and device, then setting pricing rules in Google Ad Manager (GAM) that carry higher floors on the inventory that clears higher and lower floors on the inventory that clears lower. After the change, recheck fill rate and average price across the segments to confirm the floors fit the value of each.
